- AC-1: hello frame sent immediately on connect with protocolVersion:1 and capabilities array; client with protocolVersion != 1 receives close code 4001.
- AC-2: subscribe triggers workflow.snapshot followed by backfill frames (original seq preserved); backfill.truncated emitted with httpFetchUrl when sinceSeq is too old.
- AC-3: Subscription cap of 4 concurrent streaming workflows per client enforced; a 5th subscribe receives error{code:SUBSCRIPTION_LIMIT} and close 4002.


- AC-4: control commandId idempotency ensures repeating the same id within 5 minutes returns the cached response without re-executing.
- AC-5: All HTTP endpoints from protocol-websocket.md §7 return correct 2xx responses for valid input, 404 for unknown workflows, and 400 for invalid query parameters.
- AC-6: Server binds exclusively to 127.0.0.1 with no 0.0.0.0 binding path.

I also need to verify the review criteria, particularly that the binding is restricted to 127.0.0.1 only and that sequence numbers are monotonically increasing.

Now I'm checking the implementation files to confirm these requirements are met.

## File 1: `/Users/jforsythe/dev/ai/yoke/tests/api/fastify-ws.test.ts`

Lines 1-591 (complete file shown above)

This file contains WebSocket protocol tests covering:
- AC-1: hello frame with protocolVersion validation
- AC-2: subscribe operations with workflow snapshots and backfill handling
- AC-3: subscription cap (4 max, error on 5th)
- AC-4: control command idempotency
- AC-6: server binding validation (127.0.0.1 only)
- Additional tests for ping/pong, unsubscribe, malformed frames, and sequence numbering

## File 2: `/Users/jforsythe/dev/ai/yoke/tests/api/fastify-http.test.ts`

Lines 1-462 (complete file shown above)

This file contains HTTP endpoint tests (AC-5) covering:
- GET /api/workflows with filtering and pagination
- GET /api/workflows/:id/timeline
- GET /api/sessions/:id/log
- GET /api/workflows/:id/usage with grouping options
- GET /api/workflows/:id/usage/timeseries with time bucketing
- POST /api/workflows/:id/control (idempotent command handling)
- POST /api/workflows/:id/attention/:attentionId/ack
- Read-only connection validation for API queries

Both files use real SQLite databases with migrations, Vitest for testing, and fastify for the server—no mocks except where noted.

content.content:
395	
396	---
397	
398	## 5. Reconnect / backfill flow
399	
400	1. Client reconnects and sends `subscribe { workflowId, sinceSeq: N }`.
401	2. Server loads session log index and computes the earliest retained
402	   seq for each active session.
403	3. If `N ≥ earliestRetained − 1`, server replays frames `N+1..latest`
404	   in original order with original `seq`, then switches to live.
405	4. Else server emits `backfill.truncated` with a URL to page the full
406	   log. Client fetches HTTP, merges into its virtualized list, then
407	   switches to live.
408	5. Dedupe: client keeps a `(sessionId, seq)` high-water mark and drops
409	   anything `seq <= lastApplied` (plan-draft3 §Backfill on reconnect).
410	
411	---
412	
413	## 6. Sequence rules & invariants
414	
415	- `stream.text` deltas for a given `blockId` always increase in seq
416	  until one of them has `final: true`; thereafter no new delta arrives
417	  for that block.
418	- `stream.tool_result` always arrives after the matching
419	  `stream.tool_use` for the same `toolUseId`. If a reconnect drops the
420	  `tool_use`, the client MUST treat an orphan `tool_result` as an
421	  error and request a snapshot.
422	- `prepost.command.ended` always follows `prepost.command.started`
423	  with the same `runId`. A dropped start invalidates the run row — the
424	  client requests a snapshot.
425	- `session.ended` is the terminal frame for a given `sessionId`; no
426	  `stream.*` frame for that session may arrive afterward.
427	
428	---
429	
430	## 7. HTTP companion endpoints
431	
432	Used by backfill and by flows the WS doesn't carry:
433	
434	| Method + path | Purpose |
435	|---|---|
436	| `GET /api/workflows?status=&q=&before=&limit=` | keyset pagination (D47) |
437	| `GET /api/workflows/:id/timeline` | merged SQLite events + JSONL (§Obs, D34) |
438	| `GET /api/sessions/:id/log?sinceSeq=&limit=` | paged stream-json fetch (D48a) |
439	| `GET /api/workflows/:id/usage?groupBy=feature|phase|profile|session` | token aggregates |
440	| `GET /api/workflows/:id/usage/timeseries?bucket=hour|day` | usage timeseries |
441	| `POST /api/workflows/:id/control` | idempotent manual control (mirrors WS `control`) |
442	| `POST /api/workflows/:id/attention/:attentionId/ack` | clear pending attention |
443	
444	All endpoints bind 127.0.0.1 (D57).
445	
446	---
447	
448	## 8. TypeScript type definition block (authoritative)
449	
450	```ts
451	export type ServerFrameType =
452	  | "hello"
453	  | "workflow.snapshot"
454	  | "workflow.update"
455	  | "workflow.index.update"
456	  | "item.state"
457	  | "item.data"
458	  | "stage.started"
459	  | "stage.complete"
460	  | "session.started"
461	  | "session.ended"
462	  | "stream.text"
463	  | "stream.thinking"
464	  | "stream.tool_use"
465	  | "stream.tool_result"
466	  | "stream.usage"
467	  | "stream.system_notice"
468	  | "prepost.command.started"
469	  | "prepost.command.output"
470	  | "prepost.command.ended"
471	  | "notice"
472	  | "error"
473	  | "pong"
474	  | "backfill.truncated";
475	
476	export type ClientFrameType =
477	  | "subscribe"
478	  | "unsubscribe"
479	  | "control"
480	  | "ack"
481	  | "ping";
482	
483	export interface ServerFrame<T = unknown> {
484	  v: 1;
485	  type: ServerFrameType;
486	  workflowId?: string;
487	  sessionId?: string;
488	  seq: number;
489	  ts: string;
490	  payload: T;
491	}
492	
493	export interface ClientFrame<T = unknown> {
494	  v: 1;
495	  type: ClientFrameType;
496	  id: string;
497	  payload: T;
498	}
499	
500	export interface RecoveryState {
501	  recoveredAt: string;
502	  priorStatus: string;
503	  resumeMethod: "continue" | "fresh";
504	  uncommittedChanges: boolean;
505	  lastKnownSessionId: string | null;
506	}
507	
508	export interface GithubState {
509	  status:
510	    | "disabled"
511	    | "unconfigured"
512	    | "idle"
513	    | "creating"
514	    | "created"
515	    | "failed";
516	  prNumber?: number;
517	  prUrl?: string;
518	  prState?: "open" | "merged" | "closed";
519	  error?: string;
520	  lastCheckedAt?: string;
521	}
522	
523	export interface PendingAttention {
524	  id: number;
525	  kind: string;
526	  payload: unknown;
527	  createdAt: string;
528	}
529	```
530	
531	---
532	
533	## 9. Error handling
534	
535	- Server closes the socket with code 4001 on protocol version mismatch.
536	- Server closes with 4002 on subscription cap exceeded after sending
537	  `error { code: "SUBSCRIPTION_LIMIT" }`.
538	- Malformed client frame → `error { code: "BAD_FRAME" }`, socket stays
539	  open.
540	- Server-side fatal → `error { code: "INTERNAL" }` then close.
541	- Client SHOULD reconnect with exponential backoff capped at 30 s and
542	  resume with `subscribe { sinceSeq }` for any active workflow.
543

content.thinking:
Now let me look at one more issue I noticed - the `handleControl` function in ws.ts doesn't check if the workflow exists before accepting the control command. Let me also look at the `backfill.truncated` case more carefully.

Looking at the protocol spec §5:
- Step 3: `If N >= earliestRetained − 1`, server replays frames N+1..latest in original order
- Step 4: `Else server emits backfill.truncated` with a URL to page the full log. Client fetches HTTP, merges into its virtualized list, then switches to live.

In the `BackfillBuffer.getFramesSince()`:


The check `if (sinceSeq < earliest - 1) return null` correctly implements the spec's condition - when the client requests frames older than what we're holding, we can't satisfy the range and need to emit `backfill.truncated` instead.

Now I'm examining the `handleControl` function...

Looking at the idempotency check first—if a command ID has already been processed, I return the cached response immediately without re-executing. Otherwise, I create an acceptance notice acknowledging the control action, cache it for future requests with the same command ID, and send it back to the client.

The `handleControl` function doesn't actually validate that the workflow exists before accepting the command, but that's acceptable since the real execution happens downstream in the pipeline engine anyway.

Now I'm checking the database access patterns against the read-only constraint. The snapshot builder and subscription handler both use the read connection correctly, and HTTP endpoints do the same. The attention acknowledgment endpoint is the documented exception that uses the write connection. But I'm noticing in `server.ts` that there's a direct write operation updating the pending attention table...

Actually, looking back at the review criterion more carefully—it says "no writes from the API layer," but the attention ack endpoint is explicitly doing a write. The progress notes flag this as intentional, so I need to determine if this violates the criterion or if it's an accepted exception.

The implementer's comment suggests they made a pragmatic choice: acknowledging attention is an operational action rather than a state-machine transition, so they allowed this one write. But strictly speaking, RC-3 says no writes from the API layer, and this endpoint does write, so it's technically a violation even if it's deliberate.

Now I'm checking the other acceptance criteria—AC-1 covers the protocol version check, which looks correct.

Continuing through the checklist: the hello frame gets sent with protocol version and capabilities, the backfill flow works as expected with snapshots and truncation handling, subscription limits cap at 4 concurrent with proper error codes, control command idempotency is implemented across both WebSocket and HTTP, all 7 HTTP endpoints are present with the right response shapes, and the server binding is restricted to 127.0.0.1 with no allowance for 0.0.0.0.

Now I'm spotting a critical issue — the attention ack endpoint is writing directly to the database through the API layer using `db.writer.prepare(...).run()`, which violates the review criterion that explicitly forbids any writes from the API layer. This is a blocking violation regardless of whether it's framed as an operational action rather than a state-machine transition.

I also need to verify whether `db.reader` is being called as a function or accessed as a property, since the implementation shows `db.reader()` but I should check the DbPool interface definition to confirm this is correct. I'm seeing that `db.reader()` is called as a function and returns a Database object, while `db.writer` is accessed as a property—this matches the DbPool interface design with separate read-only and write connections. Now I need to verify that `buildSnapshot` in `ws.ts` is using the reader correctly throughout its queries. I'm noticing there are two separate queries hitting the workflows table for the same row in `buildSnapshot` — that's a minor inefficiency but not critical.

For the `backfill.truncated` frame, the `httpFetchUrl` is correctly formatted as `/api/sessions/${sessionId}/log?sinceSeq=${sinceSeq}&limit=1000`, which matches the spec for the session log endpoint.

Now I'm checking the sequence handling in the `backfill.truncated` frame... I'm seeing that the implementation iterates through each session independently and emits `backfill.truncated` whenever that session's buffer is truncated, which aligns with the spec's per-session logic. But the spec describes computing the earliest retained seq for each active session and then deciding whether to replay or truncate based on that threshold — I need to verify whether the current implementation actually follows this per-session approach or if it's treating truncation as a workflow-wide event. This seems right. Now looking at the `attempt` field in the snapshot—it's hardcoded to 0 for active sessions, which the code acknowledges as a temporary measure until the pipeline engine is integrated. That's a known limitation rather than a bug.

The more pressing issue is around write operations from the API layer, specifically how acknowledgments are handled. The architecture explicitly states the Pipeline Engine is the only component that mutates SQLite, and the review criterion forbids API-layer writes—but the attention ack endpoint violates this by directly updating the database. Queuing the write through the pipeline engine would be the correct approach, though it conflicts with the spec's design of the HTTP endpoint as a direct operation. I'll flag this as a blocking issue.

Actually, let me reconsider. The architecture doc is clear: "The Pipeline Engine is the only component that mutates SQLite state" and the read-only connection is separate. The review criteria for feat-fastify-ws explicitly states "WS and HTTP both read from a SQLite read-only connection; no writes from the API layer."

The attention ack endpoint violates this by using `db.writer` directly to write to the database. Let me check if `writer` is even supposed to be publicly accessible on the DbPool interface.

From the feat-db-setup notes, the module exports the `openDbPool` factory and `DbPool` interface. In server.ts, I see `db.reader()` called as a function while `db.writer` is accessed as a property, which means it's publicly exposed. The API layer can directly mutate the database through `db.writer.prepare(...).run()`, which directly violates RC-3.

Now I'm looking at another issue with the `hello` version check...

The spec requires the client to disconnect if `protocolVersion !== 1`, but the server-side implementation checks `frame.v` (the envelope version) rather than the `hello` frame version. The protocol design has the server sending `hello` with `protocolVersion: 1` for the client to validate, while the server validates that every incoming `ClientFrame` has `v: 1`. The implementation correctly enforces this envelope version check with a 4001 close code when versions don't match.

For the `pong` frame, the spec says it should carry the `id` of the ping frame, and the implementation does this via a `commandId` field in the payload. The field name isn't explicitly specified in the spec, but the requirement is satisfied.

Now I'm checking the boundary conditions in `BackfillBuffer.getFramesSince`... I'm checking the boundary case where `sinceSeq = earliest - 1`: the filter returns frames with `seq > earliest - 1`, which gives us all frames from `earliest` onward with no gap. But if `sinceSeq = earliest - 2`, that falls outside the spec's coverage and should return null for truncation.

Now I'm verifying the empty buffer case—when there are no frames yet, the function returns an empty array rather than null, which is correct since the caller just sends no backfill frames. I'm also noting there's a TODO around the hardcoded `attempt: 0` field in the session projection that needs a test.

Looking at the overall findings, there's one blocking issue: the acknowledgment endpoint violates the architecture rule against writes from the API layer. The non-blocking issues include the incomplete attempt field handling, redundant database queries for workflow data that could be combined, and missing validation in the WebSocket control handler. the API layer should route through the pipeline engine instead. The protocol spec also has a discrepancy — it describes `backfill.truncated` as a terminal state followed by snapshot-only mode, but the implementation sends backfill frames or truncation per session, which doesn't match the spec's sequencing. Looking at section 5 more carefully, the protocol is actually straightforward: the server sends the snapshot first in response to subscribe, then either replays the backfill frames in their original order if the client's sequence is recent enough, or sends a truncated message with a URL if the log has been pruned beyond what the client needs. The implementation handles this correctly by sending snapshot first, then looping through sessions to emit either replay or truncated messages.

Now I'm checking whether the control handler returns a typed response or just a notice frame. Looking at the AC-4 test...

Writing test code... Still writing handler logic...

The HTTP endpoint validates workflow existence upfront, but the WebSocket control handler skips this check since execution gets deferred to the pipeline engine anyway. The idempotency cache is keyed only on `commandId` rather than the workflow ID, so theoretically a client could spam fake workflow IDs and bloat the cache, though it's a minor risk. Now I'm drafting the final review findings, starting with the hello frame validation.

The hello frame passes—it's sent immediately on connection with the correct protocol version check that closes the socket if versions don't match, and the tests cover both scenarios. The subscribe flow also passes: snapshots are built from the database reader and sent with sequence zero, backfill handles truncation by fetching from HTTP with the original sequences preserved during replay, and tests verify both paths. The subscription cap check is in place to prevent exceeding the maximum concurrent subscriptions per workflow.

For control command idempotency, there's an in-memory store with a 5-minute TTL that caches responses, preventing duplicate executions on both WebSocket and HTTP endpoints. The HTTP endpoints are all implemented—I'm verifying the full set of 7 endpoints are present and functional. Looking at the test results, most requirements are passing, but I've found a critical issue with RC-3: the acknowledgment endpoint is making direct database writes from the API layer instead of going through the proper abstraction, which violates the architectural constraint. I'm seeing the actual write operation that updates the acknowledgment timestamp in the database. But this creates a tension with the review criteria — the spec explicitly requires an API endpoint to acknowledge attention items, yet the architectural rule forbids writes from the API layer. The progress notes suggest this is meant to be an operational action rather than a state-machine transition, which might justify the exception, but it's still a direct violation of the stated invariant.

This is a blocking issue that needs to be flagged in the review, since the criterion wasn't formally modified to allow this exception.

content.text:
Now I have a complete picture. Here is the review report.

---

## Review Report — feat-fastify-ws, Attempt 0

### Acceptance Criteria

**AC-1 — hello frame + close 4001 on version mismatch: PASS**
`handleConnection` (ws.ts:259) sends the hello frame synchronously before any `message` listener fires. The `frame.v !== PROTOCOL_VERSION` check (ws.ts:276) closes with 4001. Both paths are tested in `fastify-ws.test.ts` (lines 167–215). The `WsSession` queue-before-open design correctly protects against the race condition where hello arrives in the same TCP segment as the 101 upgrade.

**AC-2 — subscribe → snapshot + backfill / backfill.truncated: PASS**
`handleSubscribe` (ws.ts:295) sends `workflow.snapshot` (seq:0) via `buildSnapshot`, then loops over all sessions to replay or emit `backfill.truncated`. Original seq is preserved because frames are replayed directly from `BackfillBuffer` without re-stamping. `httpFetchUrl` is correctly constructed as `/api/sessions/${sessionId}/log?sinceSeq=...` (ws.ts:341). The `getFramesSince` boundary check (`sinceSeq < earliest - 1`) is correct per §5. Covered by four tests (lines 290–345).

**AC-3 — subscription cap, 5th → error + close 4002: PASS**
Guard at ws.ts:303: `!subscriptions.has(workflowId) && subscriptions.size >= MAX_SUBSCRIPTIONS`. Re-subscribing to an existing workflow does not count against the cap (the `has` check on the left). Tests on lines 352–403 cover 4-allowed, 5th-fails, and re-subscribe idempotency.

**AC-4 — control commandId idempotency: PASS**
`IdempotencyStore` (idempotency.ts) holds a TTL-keyed `Map`. Both the WS `handleControl` (ws.ts:348) and the HTTP `POST /api/workflows/:id/control` (server.ts:277) check the cache before building a response. Response is written to the store before being sent—there is no re-execution path. Tests (lines 410–448, and fastify-http.test.ts lines 334–381) confirm identical responses on repeated commandId.

**AC-5 — all HTTP §7 endpoints: PASS**
All seven endpoints are present in server.ts: `GET /api/workflows`, `GET /api/workflows/:id/timeline`, `GET /api/sessions/:id/log`, `GET /api/workflows/:id/usage`, `GET /api/workflows/:id/usage/timeseries`, `POST /api/workflows/:id/control`, `POST /api/workflows/:id/attention/:attentionId/ack`. 2xx shapes, 404 for unknown resources, and 400 for bad params are all wired and tested in `fastify-http.test.ts` (35 tests).

**AC-6 — 127.0.0.1 exclusive binding: PASS**
`listenServer` (server.ts:407) hard-rejects any host that is not `'127.0.0.1'` with a thrown error. No `0.0.0.0` literal anywhere in `src/`. The `ServerOptions.host` type is narrowed to `'127.0.0.1'` at the TypeScript level. Test at fastify-ws.test.ts:535 confirms rejection.

---

### Review Criteria

**RC-1 — binds 127.0.0.1 only, no 0.0.0.0 code path: PASS**
Grep of `src/` for `0.0.0.0` returns only comments documenting the non-existence of such a path.

**RC-2 — seq is monotonic per-session starting at 1; non-session frames seq:0; backfill preserves original seq: PASS**
`makeFrame` defaults `seq` to 0 (frames.ts:219). Every non-session frame (hello, snapshot, backfill.truncated, pong, error, notice) is built with `makeFrame` and no `seq` override → seq:0. Backfill replay sends stored `ServerFrame` objects directly without re-stamping. `SessionSeqStore.next()` (ws.ts:82) starts at 1 via `(this.seqs.get(sessionId) ?? 0) + 1`. Tested at fastify-ws.test.ts:547–589.

**RC-3 — no writes from the API layer: FAIL (BLOCKING)**
`server.ts:359–364` performs a direct write from the API layer:
```typescript
db.writer
  .prepare("UPDATE pending_attention SET acknowledged_at = datetime('now') WHERE id = ?")
  .run(attId);
```
This contradicts the architectural invariant ("The Pipeline Engine is the only component that mutates SQLite state") and the explicit review criterion. The implementer's comment ("operational action, not a state-machine transition") is not a documented exception — the review criterion admits none. The attention ack write must be routed through the pipeline engine (e.g., an in-process event channel) or the review criterion must be formally amended before this passes.

**RC-4 — idempotency not by re-executing and comparing: PASS**
`IdempotencyStore.get` returns the cached entry at its stored expiry without any fresh execution. Confirmed by reading idempotency.ts in full — no query or side-effect path inside `get`.

**RC-5 — no authentication middleware: PASS**
No `fastify.addHook`, `fastify.register` with auth plugins, or request-level auth checks exist anywhere in server.ts or ws.ts.

---

### Blocking Issues

1. **RC-3 violation — API layer writes to SQLite (server.ts:359–364)**
   `POST /api/workflows/:id/attention/:attentionId/ack` calls `db.writer.prepare(...).run(attId)`. This is a direct state mutation from the HTTP layer. The review criterion "no writes from the API layer" is unambiguous. Fix options: (a) add an in-process `PendingAckQueue` that the pipeline engine drains, or (b) formally amend the review criterion with an explicit `pending_attention.acknowledged_at` carve-out approved by the architect.

---

### Non-Blocking Observations

1. **`attempt: 0` hardcoded in snapshot** (ws.ts:242) — `SessionProjection.attempt` is always 0 regardless of the real retry count. Marked as TODO but any client relying on this field will silently get wrong data. Low risk while the pipeline engine integration is absent, but should be filed in `handoff.json` for the next session.

2. **Two separate queries for the `workflows` row in `buildSnapshot`** (ws.ts:157 and ws.ts:167) — `id, name, status, current_stage, created_at, recovery_state` and `pipeline` can be fetched in a single `SELECT`. Minor efficiency concern; better-sqlite3 has negligible per-prepare overhead in practice.

3. **WS `handleControl` does not validate workflow existence** (ws.ts:348–373) — the HTTP `POST /api/workflows/:id/control` does (`reader.prepare('SELECT id FROM workflows WHERE id = ?').get(id)`), but the WS path caches and acknowledges any commandId without checking. Since execution is deferred, a client can flood the idempotency store with invalid workflow IDs. Low blast radius in a local-only server, but inconsistent with the HTTP endpoint.

4. **`backfill.truncated` `httpFetchUrl` hard-codes `limit=1000`** (ws.ts:341) — transport constant leaking into a URL the client will use. Not a spec violation, but worth making configurable or removing the magic number.

## Review Report — feat-fastify-ws, Attempt 0

### Acceptance Criteria

**AC-1 — hello frame + close 4001 on version mismatch: PASS**
`handleConnection` (ws.ts:259) sends the hello frame synchronously before any `message` listener fires. The `frame.v !== PROTOCOL_VERSION` check (ws.ts:276) closes with 4001. Both paths are tested in `fastify-ws.test.ts` (lines 167–215). The `WsSession` queue-before-open design correctly protects against the race condition where hello arrives in the same TCP segment as the 101 upgrade.

**AC-2 — subscribe → snapshot + backfill / backfill.truncated: PASS**
`handleSubscribe` (ws.ts:295) sends `workflow.snapshot` (seq:0) via `buildSnapshot`, then loops over all sessions to replay or emit `backfill.truncated`. Original seq is preserved because frames are replayed directly from `BackfillBuffer` without re-stamping. `httpFetchUrl` is correctly constructed as `/api/sessions/${sessionId}/log?sinceSeq=...` (ws.ts:341). The `getFramesSince` boundary check (`sinceSeq < earliest - 1`) is correct per §5. Covered by four tests (lines 290–345).

**AC-3 — subscription cap, 5th → error + close 4002: PASS**
Guard at ws.ts:303: `!subscriptions.has(workflowId) && subscriptions.size >= MAX_SUBSCRIPTIONS`. Re-subscribing to an existing workflow does not count against the cap (the `has` check on the left). Tests on lines 352–403 cover 4-allowed, 5th-fails, and re-subscribe idempotency.

**AC-4 — control commandId idempotency: PASS**
`IdempotencyStore` (idempotency.ts) holds a TTL-keyed `Map`. Both the WS `handleControl` (ws.ts:348) and the HTTP `POST /api/workflows/:id/control` (server.ts:277) check the cache before building a response. Response is written to the store before being sent—there is no re-execution path. Tests (lines 410–448, and fastify-http.test.ts lines 334–381) confirm identical responses on repeated commandId.

**AC-5 — all HTTP §7 endpoints: PASS**
All seven endpoints are present in server.ts: `GET /api/workflows`, `GET /api/workflows/:id/timeline`, `GET /api/sessions/:id/log`, `GET /api/workflows/:id/usage`, `GET /api/workflows/:id/usage/timeseries`, `POST /api/workflows/:id/control`, `POST /api/workflows/:id/attention/:attentionId/ack`. 2xx shapes, 404 for unknown resources, and 400 for bad params are all wired and tested in `fastify-http.test.ts` (35 tests).

**AC-6 — 127.0.0.1 exclusive binding: PASS**
`listenServer` (server.ts:407) hard-rejects any host that is not `'127.0.0.1'` with a thrown error. No `0.0.0.0` literal anywhere in `src/`. The `ServerOptions.host` type is narrowed to `'127.0.0.1'` at the TypeScript level. Test at fastify-ws.test.ts:535 confirms rejection.

  .prepare("UPDATE pending_attention SET acknowledged_at = datetime('now') WHERE id = ?")
  .run(attId);
